subject: ap-psychology
theme: The biological bases of behavior encompass the structure and function of neurons, neural communication, major brain regions, the two hemispheres, and how genetics and evolution shape psychological traits.

core_concepts.title: Neurons & the Action Potential
core_concepts.detail: Neurons transmit signals via the action potential — an all-or-none electrical impulse triggered when depolarization exceeds threshold. Neurotransmitters are released at the synapse and bind to receptors on the postsynaptic neuron, producing excitatory or inhibitory effects.

core_concepts.title: Central & Peripheral Nervous Systems
core_concepts.detail: The central nervous system (brain + spinal cord) integrates information; the peripheral nervous system (somatic + autonomic) connects the CNS to the body. The autonomic system's sympathetic division mobilizes the fight-or-flight response; the parasympathetic division restores rest-and-digest.

core_concepts.title: Major Brain Structures
core_concepts.detail: Major brain structures: the brainstem (medulla, pons, reticular formation) controls survival functions and arousal; the cerebellum coordinates movement and procedural memory; the limbic system (hippocampus = memory consolidation, amygdala = fear/emotion, hypothalamus = drives) regulates motivation and emotion; the cerebral cortex handles higher cognition.

core_concepts.title: Brain Lateralization
core_concepts.detail: Brain lateralization: left hemisphere governs language (Broca's area = speech production; Wernicke's area = language comprehension) and analytic processing; right hemisphere handles spatial tasks, facial recognition, and holistic processing. The corpus callosum connects and coordinates the two hemispheres.

core_concepts.title: Brain Plasticity
core_concepts.detail: Brain plasticity means the nervous system reorganizes through experience — synaptic pruning, neurogenesis (hippocampus), and functional reorganization after injury. Plasticity is greatest in early development but continues throughout life.

core_concepts.title: Behavioral Genetics
core_concepts.detail: Behavioral genetics studies heredity's role using twin studies (MZ vs DZ comparisons), adoption studies, and heritability estimates. Most psychological traits reflect gene-environment interaction — genes set reaction ranges that environments shape.

core_concepts.title: Key Neurotransmitter Systems
core_concepts.detail: Key neurotransmitter systems: dopamine (reward, movement — deficient in Parkinson's, excess linked to schizophrenia), serotonin (mood, sleep — targeted by SSRIs), GABA (inhibitory — targeted by benzodiazepines), acetylcholine (muscle control, memory — blocked in Alzheimer's), norepinephrine (arousal, fight-or-flight).

exam_tip: FRQ case studies require naming a specific brain structure or neurotransmitter AND explaining its function — partial credit is lost if you only name the structure without explaining the mechanism.
